The **NSW Cup**, currently known as the **Knock-On Effect NSW Cup** for sponsorship reasons, is a [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") competition for clubs in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"). The competition has a history dating back to the [NSWRFL](/wiki/NSWRFL "NSWRFL")'s origins in 1908, starting off as a reserve grade competition, and is now the premier open age competition in the state. The **New South Wales Cup**, along with the **[Queensland Cup](/wiki/Queensland_Cup "Queensland Cup")**, acts as a feeder competition to the **[National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League")** premiership. The competition is the oldest continuous rugby league competition in Australia.

The NSW Cup is contested by reserve squads of NSW-based NRL teams and also includes sides representing teams that once competed at the first grade level in the NSWRL Premiership but no longer field teams in the NRL competition, and teams that have not fielded teams in the NRL competition. The [North Sydney Bears](/wiki/North_Sydney_Bears "North Sydney Bears") are the only team to have competed in every season, since the start of the competition since 1908.

## Clubs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Clubs")]

### New South Wales Cup teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: New South Wales Cup teams")]

The New South Wales Cup consists of 13 teams, 1 each from [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland_Region "Auckland Region") and [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory"), and 11 from [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"). The league operates on a single group system, with no divisions or conferences and no relegation and promotion from other leagues. A number of clubs in the New South Wales Cup have an affiliation with a team in the Australian national competition, the National Rugby League, with most of the clubs in the being reserve grade teams to the senior grade teams of the NRL.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/6/67/NSW_Cup_Logo_until_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:NSW_Cup_Logo_until_2012.jpg)

NSW Cup Logo until 2012

The NSW Cup was known as the **Reserve Grade/Presidents Cup/First Division** from 1908 until 2002, and the **NSWRL Premier League** from 2003 to 2007, the **NSW Cup** from 2008 to 2015, the **Intrust Super Premiership NSW** from 2016 to 2018, the **Canterbury Cup NSW** from 2019 to 2020. The competition's lineage follows that of the NSWRL Reserve Grade from 1908 onwards.

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 20th century")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. The New South Wales Cup, run by the [NSWRL](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League "New South Wales Rugby League"), has been known by a variety of names and operated in several different ways since the inception of the [NSWRL Premiership](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League_premiership "New South Wales Rugby League premiership") in 1908. Between 1908 and 1996, the competition was known as **Reserve Grade** and was competed for almost exclusively by reserve squads of each of the NSWRL Premiership Clubs, competing with that Club's name and colours. With the advent of the [Super League war](/wiki/Super_League_war "Super League war"), and the resultant split competition in 1997, the NSWRL reconfigured the competition as the **Presidents Cup**. From 2002 until 2007, the competition was known as the **NSWRL Premier League** until it was reorganised into its present form as the **New South Wales Cup** in 2008.

### 'Stand-alone' clubs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 'Stand-alone' clubs")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/NSW_Cup "Special:EditPage/NSW Cup") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(May 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

With the competitions having merged back together, and with six [NSWRL Premiership](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League_premiership "New South Wales Rugby League premiership") clubs having merged or became joint-ventures into three new NRL clubs ([St. George Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Dragons "St. George Dragons") and [Illawarra Steelers](/wiki/Illawarra_Steelers "Illawarra Steelers"); [North Sydney](/wiki/North_Sydney_Bears "North Sydney Bears") and [Manly](/wiki/Manly-Warringah_Sea_Eagles "Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles"); [Balmain Tigers](/wiki/Balmain_Tigers "Balmain Tigers") and [Western Suburbs](/wiki/Western_Suburbs_Magpies "Western Suburbs Magpies")) the competition became known as the **First Division** and included these sides competing under their original name and colours.

The inclusion of these non-NRL clubs (along with the return of the [Newtown Jets](/wiki/Newtown_Jets "Newtown Jets") in 2000) in the competition signalled a move away from the 'reserve squad' competition it had become and became increasingly differentiated from the NRL competition with games played at non-NRL venues such as [North Sydney Oval](/wiki/North_Sydney_Oval "North Sydney Oval"), Marrickville's [Henson Park](/wiki/Henson_Park "Henson Park") and [Western Weekender Stadium](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Saints "St Mary's Saints") at St Marys.

Another trend that began during this period was the phenomenon of NRL clubs outsourcing competing teams, with several NRL clubs choosing not to field sides in this competition and rather field either merged entities (as in the [St Marys Penrith Cougars](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Saints "St Mary's Saints") and [Balmain Ryde Eastwood Tigers](/wiki/Balmain_Ryde_Eastwood_Tigers "Balmain Ryde Eastwood Tigers"), both formed with [NSWRL Jim Beam Cup](/wiki/NSWRL_Jim_Beam_Cup "NSWRL Jim Beam Cup") sides) or form agreements with another club to take their place in the competition, those players being eligible for NRL selection, such as the agreement between [Newtown Jets](/wiki/Newtown_Jets "Newtown Jets") and [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") for the 2006 season.

### NSW Cup Era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: NSW Cup Era")]

#### 2007–09 Expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2007–09 Expansion")]

In 2007, [Bartercard Cup](/wiki/Bartercard_Cup "Bartercard Cup") club [Auckland Lions](/wiki/Auckland_Lions "Auckland Lions") joined the competition.

In 2008 and 2009, [Jersey Flegg Cup](/wiki/Jersey_Flegg_Cup "Jersey Flegg Cup") club Central Coast Storm fielded a team in the competition. The team was based on the NSW Central Coast but acted as a feeder club to the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm").[[2]](#cite_note-3)
In addition, the Panthers were replaced by the [Windsor Wolves](/wiki/Windsor_Wolves "Windsor Wolves") and the Sharks were replaced by the [Cronulla-Sutherland Cobras](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Cobras "Cronulla-Sutherland Cobras"). The Canberra Raiders withdrew from the competition on 1 August 2007. The [Newcastle Knights](/wiki/Newcastle_Knights "Newcastle Knights") also announced a joint venture with the Central Charlestown. The team used the original [Central Newcastle Rebels](/wiki/Newcastle_Rebels "Newcastle Rebels") Name.[[3]](#cite_note-4) The [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") also formed a joint-venture with the [Wentworthville Magpies](/wiki/Wentworthville_Magpies "Wentworthville Magpies") to act as their Feeder Club in the competition from 2008 onwards.[[4]](#cite_note-5) The [Saints](/wiki/St_George-Illawarra_Dragons "St George-Illawarra Dragons") decided to no longer run a Reserve Grade Side, but would use the St George District Rugby League & the Illawarra District Rugby League competitions instead as their Feeder Team/s.

Two new teams have been added to the competition. These two new teams will have both previously played in the [Jim Beam Cup](/wiki/Jim_Beam_Cup "Jim Beam Cup"). The Shellharbour City Dragons, previously known as the Shellharbour Marlins, will be the [St George-Illawarra Dragons](/wiki/St_George-Illawarra_Dragons "St George-Illawarra Dragons") feeder side. The Bankstown Bulls, who were known as the Sydney Bulls, will act as the [Canterbury Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs") feeder side. Bankstown will still field a team in the [Jim Beam Cup](/wiki/Jim_Beam_Cup "Jim Beam Cup"). The [Manly Sea Eagles](/wiki/Manly-Warringah_Sea_Eagles "Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles") have withdrawn from the competition and will have a feeder team in the [Queensland Cup](/wiki/Queensland_Cup "Queensland Cup"). Newcastle had also withdrawn from the competition, discontinuing the link with the [Central Newcastle Rebels](/wiki/Newcastle_Rebels "Newcastle Rebels").

#### 2010–12 Seasons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2010–12 Seasons")]

Season 2012 saw the return of feeder clubs for NRL teams St George Illawarra and Canberra. The Illawarra Steelers, in partnership with Illawarra Coal and the Illawarra Leagues Club re-entered a team into the league, the Illawarra Cutters. They previously acted as a feeder club to the Dragons. A Mounties Rugby League Club also entered the NSW Cup this season and is the Raiders' feeder club.

#### 2013–15 Expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2013–15 Expansion")]

The 2013 season saw Wyong Roos enter a team in the NSW Cup for the first time. It will not be a feeder team to any NRL team.
2013 was also the first time in Rugby League history that teams with the names Western Suburbs and Balmain would not field a team in the cup, they played as the Wests Tigers. There is a current state of ambiguity surrounding this joint venture, and it is suggested that both Wests and Balmain will return as two separate clubs once financial requirements are met.

In 2014 the [Auckland Vulcans](/wiki/Auckland_Vulcans "Auckland Vulcans") were replaced by a side from the [New Zealand Warriors](/wiki/Warriors_(NRL) "Warriors (NRL)").[[5]](#cite_note-6) The Penrith Panthers will also be returning to the competition in 2014, replacing Windsor, who remain in the Ron Massey Cup.

#### 2016–18: Intrust Super Premiership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2016–18: Intrust Super Premiership")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/a/a1/Intrust_Super_Premiership_Logo.png)](/wiki/File:Intrust_Super_Premiership_Logo.png)

Intrust Super Premiership Logo from 2016 to 2018

On 29 January 2016, it was announced that Intrust Super had secured naming rights for the competition for a three-year agreement[[6]](#cite_note-7) The name would have been decided not to be confused with the Queensland-based competition the [Intrust Super Cup](/wiki/Queensland_Cup "Queensland Cup").

On 5 July 2016, it was announced that starting the following season, the Blacktown Workers would become the feeder club for the Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles in a joint-venture agreement.[[7]](#cite_note-8) The Blacktown Workers Sea Eagles made their NSW Cup debut in Round 1 of the 2017 Season with a defeat to the Newtown Jets, before finishing 10th in the regular season and subsequently missing a post-season berth.

In September, Intrust Super extended its naming rights partnership with the New South Wales State Cup through to the end of the 2018 season.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

On 27 October 2017, it was announced that Illawarra would be replaced by The St George Illawarra Dragons for The 2018 Intrust Super Premiership NSW season as part of a restructure in the competition.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

In late November 2017 it was announced that as part of a re-brand, the Western Suburbs Magpies would enter the competition from the following season acting as a feeder club to the Wests Tigers, who had previously competed under their own brand.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

#### 2019–2020: Canterbury Cup NSW

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2019–2020: Canterbury Cup NSW")]

On 2 March 2018, it was reported that the board of the [Wyong Roos](/wiki/Wyong_Roos "Wyong Roos"), feeder to [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") since 2014, voted to cut all ties with the club at end of the 2018 season.[[11]](#cite_note-12) As a result, the Wyong Roos did not take part in the 2019 Intrust Super Premiership. On 5 September 2018, it was announced that the [North Sydney Bears](/wiki/North_Sydney_Bears "North Sydney Bears") would assume the status as the official feeder club to the Roosters NRL side[[12]](#cite_note-13) until at least the end of the 2023 season, with [Jason Taylor](/wiki/Jason_Taylor_(rugby_league) "Jason Taylor (rugby league)"), a former North Sydney player and assistant coach to the Roosters, appointed head coach.[[13]](#cite_note-14) Taylor, after leading the Bears to a third-place finish at the end of the regular season, was announced to have signed a two-year extension on 5 November 2019.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

On 7 March 2019, it was announced that apparel company [Canterbury of New Zealand](/wiki/Canterbury_of_New_Zealand "Canterbury of New Zealand") won the rights to be the new naming partner of the NSW Cup competition which was renamed the Canterbury Cup NSW. The deal was announced to run to the end of the 2024 season. The NSWRL also announced that the new [Western Sydney Stadium](/wiki/Western_Sydney_Stadium "Western Sydney Stadium") would host the grand final in each of those seasons under the deal.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

[South Sydney](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs"), having had a previous feeder relationship with North Sydney, would then field their own team in the Intrust Super Premiership,[[16]](#cite_note-17) keeping the number of competing teams at 12.

On 10 October 2018, it was reported that the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") would field a team in the Intrust Super Premiership starting 2020, thus ending their relationship with the [Wentworthville Magpies](/wiki/Wentworthville_Magpies "Wentworthville Magpies") at this time.[[17]](#cite_note-18) It was announced that former Wyong Roos coach, Rip Taylor, would coach the Magpies in their final season.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

On Friday, 27 March 2020, after round one of the season was completed, the 2020 Canterbury Cup NSW competition was suspended, and subsequently cancelled due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), with no premiers being crowned.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

#### 2021–present: Knock-on Effect NSW Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 2021–present: Knock-on Effect NSW Cup")]

On 8 June 2020, the [New Zealand Warriors](/wiki/Warriors_(NRL) "Warriors (NRL)") and [Redcliffe Dolphins](/wiki/Redcliffe_Dolphins "Redcliffe Dolphins") announced a partnership agreement, effectively withdrawing the Warriors from the Canterbury Cup competition. Through this initiative a number of players from outside the Warriors’ NRL squad will appear for the Dolphins in the Intrust Super Cup each week.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

On 28 August 2020, the [Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs") announced a joint venture with the [Mount Pritchard Mounties](/wiki/Mount_Pritchard_Mounties "Mount Pritchard Mounties") for two years.[[21]](#cite_note-22) As part of the joint venture, the Mounties will represent Canterbury-Bankstown in the Canterbury Cup, ending their nine-year relationship with the [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders"). At the time the Canberra Raiders were set to go it alone in 2021 rather than form an affiliation with a NSW Cup side[[22]](#cite_note-23)

On 10 November 2020, the NSWRL confirmed that the NSW Cup would return in 2021 with a 10-team competition, however Canterbury would no longer hold naming rights. A new naming rights partner was revealed prior to the season launch on 3 March 2021.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

On 28 January 2021, it was announced that the [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders") would be returning to the competition, fielding their own team or the first time since the 2007 season, thus increasing the number of competing teams to 11 in 2021. The competition commenced on 13 March 2021.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

On 3 March 2021, a new naming rights sponsor, The Knock-On Effect, was named at the launch of the 2021 season. The new partnership will be in place for the next three years replacing the previous sponsor, Canterbury NZ.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

For the second consecutive year, the competition was cancelled on 10 August 2021, after completion of 15 out of 24 scheduled rounds, due to the ongoing [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[26]](#cite_note-27)

On 29 October 2021, it was announced that the [Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs") would be running their own team in the NSW Cup in 2022, in addition to continuing their partnership with Mounties for a further season due to contractual obligations[[27]](#cite_note-28)

The draw for the 2022 season was released on 17 December 2021, and announced a revision to the finals-system to a top five, a decrease from the previous top eight.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

On 17 August 2022, the [New Zealand Warriors](/wiki/Warriors_(NRL) "Warriors (NRL)") announced they would be re-joining the NSW Cup after a three-year absence, ending their partnership with the [Redcliffe Dolphins](/wiki/Redcliffe_Dolphins "Redcliffe Dolphins").[[29]](#cite_note-30)

On 9 November 2022, the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") announced they would field their own team in the NSW Cup commencing in the 2023 season, ending their partnership with the [North Sydney Bears](/wiki/North_Sydney_Bears "North Sydney Bears") from 2024 onwards.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

On 20 September 2024, the [Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles](/wiki/Manly_Warringah_Sea_Eagles "Manly Warringah Sea Eagles") announced they would field their own team in the NSW Cup, commencing in the 2025 season. This ended their 9-year partnership with the [Blacktown Workers](/wiki/Blacktown_Workers "Blacktown Workers") that was established in 2016.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

## Broadcast & Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Broadcast & Media")]

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Television")]

The 2025 season broadcast deal features:

* One game per week on [Fox League](/wiki/Fox_League "Fox League")/[Kayo Sports](/wiki/Kayo_Sports "Kayo Sports") at either Saturday or Sunday 12:30pm or 11:45am
* Every Game Live on BarTV Sports.
* The Grand Final Live on [Nine Now](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network").

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Radio")]

[Hawkesbury Radio](/wiki/Hawkesbury_Radio "Hawkesbury Radio") broadcast matches of the [Penrith Panthers](/wiki/Penrith_Panthers "Penrith Panthers") online during the season when played on non-NRL game days, selected other matches and all the finals matches.

[Triple H Radio](/wiki/Triple_H_(Sydney) "Triple H (Sydney)") broadcast selected matches of the [North Sydney Bears](/wiki/North_Sydney_Bears "North Sydney Bears") during the year plus selected finals matches.

There is also additional radio coverage of the finals series on [2GB Radio](/wiki/2GB "2GB") and [702 ABC Sydney](/wiki/702_ABC_Sydney "702 ABC Sydney").

### Online

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Online")]

The NSWRL website upload highlights of every game of the NSW Cup. It also gives half time and full-time scores of the other games.

All NSW Cup matches are shown LIVE on NSWRL TV via BarTV Sports.

## NRL State Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NSW_Cup&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: NRL State Championship")]

Main article: [NRL State Championship](/wiki/NRL_State_Championship "NRL State Championship")

Since 2014, The NSW Cup Grand Final has been played on the same weekend as the Queensland Cup Grand Final, the weekend prior to the NRL Grand Final, allowing for the creation of the [NRL State Championship](/wiki/NRL_State_Championship "NRL State Championship") which saw the NSW Cup premiers face off against the Queensland Cup Premiers as a curtain raiser to the [NRL Grand Final](/wiki/NRL_Grand_Final "NRL Grand Final"), originally following the [National Youth Competition](/wiki/NRL_Under-20s "NRL Under-20s") Grand Final from 2014 to 2017[[107]](#cite_note-109)[[108]](#cite_note-110) and following the NRL Women's Grand Final in their inaugural premiership year in 2018.

In 2019 the NRL State Championship was played prior the NRL Women's Grand Final. Newtown Jets became the fourth NSWRL team to win in as many years after the first two championships were won by the QRL.

The 2020 State Championship was cancelled due to the Queensland and New South Wales competitions being cancelled after Round 1 due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and the 2021 State Championship was cancelled due to the COVID-19 lockdown in Sydney
